content.text: *"In my Father's house are many rooms."*{John 14:2}{If more pigeons than pigeonholes exist, some hole must contain more than one pigeon.}{Peter Gustav Lejeune Dirichlet}

Several powerful techniques extend basic counting: inclusion-exclusion, 
the pigeonhole principle, and generating functions.

content.title: Inclusion-Exclusion
content.content:
For finite sets $A_1, A_2, \ldots, A_n$:
$$
|A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \cdots \cup A_n| &= \sum_i |A_i| - \sum_{i < j} |A_i \cap A_j| \\
&+ \sum_{i < j < k} |A_i \cap A_j \cap A_k| - \cdots \\
&+ (-1)^{n+1} |A_1 \cap A_2 \cap \cdots \cap A_n|
$$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Counting derangements
content.problem:
A **derangement** is a permutation where no 
element remains in its original position.

For $n$ elements, the number of derangements is:
\[ D_n = n! \sum_{k=0}^{n} \frac{(-1)^k}{k!} \approx \frac{n!}{e} \]

$D_4 = 24(1 - 1 + 1/2 - 1/6 + 1/24) = 24 \cdot 9/24 = 9$.

content.title: Pigeonhole Principle
content.content:
If $n$ items are placed into $k$ containers and $n > k$, then at least 
one container has more than one item.

**Generalized:** If $n$ items go into $k$ containers, at least one 
container has $\lceil n/k \rceil$ or more items.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Birthday problem
content.problem:
In any group of 367 people, at least two share a birthday.

(There are at most 366 distinct birthdays.)

content.title: Subset sums
content.problem:
From any 10 integers, some nonempty subset has sum divisible by 10.

proof
Consider partial sums $S_0 = 0$, $S_1 = a_1$, $S_2 = a_1 + a_2$, …, 
$S_{10} = a_1 + \cdots + a_{10}$.

These 11 sums have remainders mod 10 in $\{0, 1, \ldots, 9\}$ (10 values).

By pigeonhole, two have the same remainder: $S_i \equiv S_j \pmod{10}$ 
for some $i < j$.

Then $S_j - S_i = a_{i+1} + \cdots + a_j$ is divisible by 10.
proof

content.title: Stars and Bars
content.content:
The number of ways to distribute $n$ identical objects into $k$ distinct 
bins is $\binom{n + k - 1}{k - 1}$.

With at least one object per bin: $\binom{n - 1}{k - 1}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Distributing candy
content.problem:
How many ways can 10 identical candies be distributed among 4 children?

$\binom{10 + 4 - 1}{4 - 1} = \binom{13}{3} = 286$.

marginNotes.text:
Lift up your eyes and look to the heavens: 
Who created all these? He who brings out the starry host one by one and 
calls forth each of them by name.
